cards.title: Google Integrates Computer Use Natively into Gemini 3.5 Flash
cards.why_it_matters: Gemini 3.5 Flash is now a single API endpoint for function calling, search grounding, and full computer use — a meaningful consolidation for anyone building multi-step agents.
cards.summary: Computer use — previously a standalone Gemini 2.5 model — is now a built-in tool in Gemini 3.5 Flash, accessible via the Gemini API and Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform. Safety mitigations include adversarial training against prompt injection, optional user-confirmation gates for irreversible actions, and automatic task-stop on detected indirect injection. Targets browser, mobile, and desktop automation for long-horizon enterprise tasks.

cards.title: NVIDIA NeMo AutoModel Delivers 3.4–3.7x MoE Fine-Tuning Throughput Over Native Transformers v5
cards.why_it_matters: If you fine-tune MoE models, this is a drop-in upgrade — one import swap gives you Expert Parallelism, DeepEP fused all-to-all dispatch, and TransformerEngine kernels with no other code changes.
cards.summary: NeMo AutoModel subclasses HuggingFace AutoModelForCausalLM and layers Expert Parallelism, DeepEP fused all-to-all communication-compute overlap, and TransformerEngine kernels on top of Transformers v5. Measured gains vs. native v5: 3.4–3.7x training throughput and 29–32% less GPU memory for MoE fine-tuning. Tested from single-node Qwen3-30B-A3B up to 16-node Nemotron Ultra 550B. Checkpoints remain standard HF format, compatible with vLLM and SGLang.

cards.title: FFASR Leaderboard: First Open Far-Field ASR Benchmark Exposes Large Real-World Performance Gap
cards.why_it_matters: If you deploy speech models in any non-headset environment — robots, conference rooms, in-car — this benchmark directly quantifies the degradation you'll encounter and lets you compare models on Pareto WER vs. real-time-factor tradeoffs.
cards.summary: Treble Technologies and Hugging Face launched the Far-Field ASR leaderboard, evaluating models across four primary conditions: near-field dry, far-field high SNR (>14 dB), mid SNR (8–12 dB), and low SNR (<6 dB) — simulated across 14 rooms validated against real measurements. Submitted models show far-field WER at low SNR consistently several times higher than near-field WER. Roadmap includes multi-talker, microphone arrays, and echo cancellation.

cards.title: Op-Ed: Why Banning Open-Source AI Would Harm Education, Competition, and Innovation
cards.why_it_matters: As U.S. policy moves toward broader AI regulation — including an executive order reviewing AI models and restrictions on foreign nationals accessing frontier models — this piece articulates the technical-community case for keeping open-source AI legal.
cards.summary: Co-authored by Nathan Lambert and Kevin Xu, the op-ed argues that open-source AI regulation would undermine three decades of education, innovation, and competition enabled by open software. It cites open source underpinning over 90% of global software and generating over $8 trillion in economic value. Written for a general audience after being rejected by mainstream outlets; authors are self-publishing to reach undecided readers.

cards.title: Pretraining VLA Action Modules on Motion Priors Improves Cross-Embodiment Robot Policies
cards.why_it_matters: For researchers working on robot learning or VLA architectures, this two-stage flow-matching pretraining approach directly addresses the cold-start problem for action modules and shows scaling the action pretraining data improves downstream VLA performance.
cards.summary: The paper proposes pretraining a flow-matching encoder-decoder action module on unconditioned action trajectories before VLA training, then transferring it via decoder reuse and early-stage latent distillation. The pretrained encoder also compresses state-action history into a single temporal context token. Validated on 13 cross-embodiment tasks in simulation and real-world settings; gains are especially pronounced in data-scarce real-world conditions.

cards.title: RevengeBench: Can LLMs Reverse-Engineer Hidden Agent Policies from Behavioral Traces?
cards.why_it_matters: This benchmark formalizes policy interpretability as a code-reconstruction inverse problem — relevant for anyone working on opponent modeling, agent interpretability, or using LLMs as scientific reasoners.
cards.summary: RevengeBench contains 75 Elo-calibrated LLM-generated policies across five game environments from CodeClash tournaments. A learner observes behavioral traces, designs probing opponent policies, then submits executable code hypotheses evaluated via continuous action-distance metrics. Across 12 frontier LLMs, recovery quality ranges from 34% to 72% of initial action distance closed. Recovered policies show measurable competitive advantage, especially for weaker baseline models.

cards.title: On-Policy Self-Distillation Trades Output Diversity for Pass@1 Accuracy — Hurting Pass@k and OOD Robustness
cards.why_it_matters: If you're using self-distillation with sampled demonstrations as an alternative to RL for post-training, this paper shows it silently collapses output diversity, which matters for best-of-N inference, ensembling, or any setting requiring diverse reasoning strategies.
cards.summary: Self-distillation where the teacher is conditioned on a correct demonstration tilts the learned distribution by a pointwise conditional mutual information score, amplifying probability gaps between rollouts rather than preserving them as on-policy RL does. Empirically, self-distilled models match or exceed RL on average accuracy but show substantially lower functional and semantic diversity, with flattened pass@k curves and failures on OOD tasks requiring diverse strategies.

cards.title: Quantum Gibbs Samplers Achieve System-Size-Independent Spectral Gap for All-to-All k-Local Hamiltonians at High Temperature
cards.why_it_matters: This extends quantum fast-mixing guarantees beyond geometrically-local lattice systems to arbitrary all-to-all connected Hamiltonians — a meaningful theoretical step for quantum ML and quantum simulation applications involving non-local interactions.
cards.summary: The paper proves that quantum k-local Hamiltonians with bounded-strength interactions admit a quantum Gibbs sampler with a spectral gap independent of system size at sufficiently high temperatures, generalizing prior fast-mixing results from geometrically-local to all-to-all settings. As a corollary, such systems have fully-polynomial time quantum approximation algorithms for partition functions and global expectation values.
